key_insight: Three commercial multimodal large language models showed non-deployment-ready performance on clinically actionable breast pathology tasks, with each vendor failing in a distinct way.
clinical_relevance: These models may help as supervised draft generators, but they should not be used autonomously for treatment-determining breast cancer features such as subtype, grade, or HER2 status.
method: Evaluated Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and GPT-5.5 on 427 invasive breast cancer cases.
Each case used identical H&E image tiles and prompts, with subtype inferred in a second call.
Compared model outputs against institutional sign-out reports using concordance, sensitivity, specificity, kappa, and pairwise tests.
evidence_signal: Across 4,056 prompt variants, none of the 12 prompt settings rescued sensitivity, and vendor-specific error patterns persisted across histologic type, grade, and HER2 prediction.
watch_item: Changing vendors changes the direction of error rather than eliminating it, so local validation and human oversight remain essential before any workflow use.
paper_title: Three multimodal large language models fail at clinically actionable breast pathology in three different directions
